text: His main point seems to be that external DSLs are bad, but writing declarative style code can be good and composable. The embedded HTML example he gives reminds me a lot of how the HTML module works in Elm. <https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/elm-lang/html/latest/Html>

His main point seems to be that external DSLs are bad, but writing declarative style code can be good and composable. The embedded HTML example he gives reminds me a lot of how the HTML module works in Elm. <https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/elm-lang/html/latest/Html>

Here is one-on-one talk between me and <@UFD2JRE58>

discussed:
 * what are real problems? efficient abstractions, pain of macros
 * consistency in system with many parts on different platforms
 * how wind turbines work, embedded programming
 * system of code generators from spreadsheets, LadderLogic
 * realtime systems
 * testing of embedded systems
 * process of program design
 * vague description, constraints
 * structured documentation that puts constraints on code
 * <https://thorny.io/>, space of decisions, way to capture reasoning
 * different personalities, different tools?
 * me (Vladimir Gordeev) preparing for university

<https://youtu.be/UagX5ssqaVM>

> code AST generally removes all formatting info

I'm not sure how true this is in the real world. The AST libraries I have written for myself and those I have used (e.g. Roslyn) retain text-span information so you can navigate to/from the source code, display warnings on the right characters,  perform refactorings etc.

> code AST generally removes all formatting info

I'm not sure how true this is in the real world. The AST libraries I have written for myself and those I have used (e.g. Roslyn) retain text-span information so you can navigate to/from the source code, display warnings on the right characters,  perform refactorings etc.

I realized yesterday how similar Observable notebooks are to visual (graph based) programming: in Observable, any time you change a value somewhere, all references using it update reactively.

<https://beta.observablehq.com/@mbostock/how-observable-runs>
<https://twitter.com/jaukia/status/1093571831607513089>

Interesting how different UIs for same level of programming abstraction lead to quite different UX.

text:
I’m currently designing a protocol which captures intent, knowledge, and context for each fact entered into a relational database, but I’m not satisfied with my vocabulary for describing what is actually important in the sense of (tracking) credibility of entries and observations made by different actors. I’m thinking about domains of control/permission and authority over subject matters, but am struggling with language.
Would anyone recommend any specific materials, projects, or papers that talk towards these topics?

<@UC2A2ARPT> I haven’t heard of RDF yet, I may be able to leverage the *subject* / *predicate* / *object* ideas to further develop my understandings. I also liked this topic <@UF5PAGQQ4> mentioned earlier: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Provenance#Computer_science>
Which has language that can be used for the transfer of knowledge. For example, if one actor says “I can see that the sky is blue” (because I know what a sky is from `x`, a color is from `y`, and the predicate from myself).
